title: Methods and apparatus for determining health status of electric actuators based on torque trend data
abstract: Methods and apparatus for determining a health status of an electric actuator are described. An example apparatus includes a controller operatively coupled to an electric actuator via a network. The controller is configured to obtain torque curve data over the network from the electric actuator, the torque curve data being associated with actuation of an electric motor of the electric actuator, the actuation to cause a flow control member of a motor-operated valve to move, the flow control member being mechanically coupled to the electric motor and being movable between an open position and a closed position. The controller is configured to determine an area under a torque curve based on the torque curve data, determine a variance between the area under the torque curve and an area under a reference curve, and, generate a first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that the variance does not exceed the variance threshold.
claims:
Claims What Is Claimed Is: 1. An apparatus for determining a health status of an electric actuator of a motor- operated valve, the apparatus comprising: a controller operatively coupled to the electric actuator via a network, the controller configured to: obtain torque curve data over the network from the electric actuator, the torque curve data being associated with actuation of an electric motor of the electric actuator, the actuation to cause a flow control member of the motor-operated valve to move, the flow control member being mechanically coupled to the electric motor and being movable between an open position and a closed position; determine an area under a torque curve based on the torque curve data; determine a variance between the area under the torque curve and an area under a reference curve; determine whether the variance exceeds a variance threshold; and generate a first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that the variance does not exceed the variance threshold. 2. The apparatus of claim 1, further comprising a user interface operatively coupled to the controller, the user interface configured to present a first notification indicating that the electric actuator is healthy, the first notification being based on the first control signal. 3. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the controller is further configured to generate a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that the variance exceeds the variance threshold. 4. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the user interface is further configured to present a second notification indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy, the second notification being based on the second control signal. 5. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the torque curve is partitioned into a breakout stage, a running stage, and an ending stage, and wherein the controller is further configured to: 

 determine the area under the torque curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determine the variance between the area under the torque curve and the area under the reference curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determine whether the variance exceeds the variance threshold separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; and generate the first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that none of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold. 6. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the controller is further configured to generate a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that any of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold. 7. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the controller is further configured to generate an information request control signal, the information request control signal to be transmitted over the network from the controller to the electric actuator to cause the electric actuator to deliver the torque curve data to the controller over the network. 8. The apparatus of any preceding claim, wherein the controller is configured to generate the information request control signal based on at least one of a time-based schedule managed by the controller, an actuation-based schedule managed by the controller, or an input received by the controller from a user interface operatively coupled to the controller. 9. A method for determining a health status of an electric actuator of a motor- operated valve, the method comprising: obtaining, by executing one or more instructions with a controller operatively coupled to the electric actuator via a network, torque curve data over the network from the electric actuator, the torque curve data being associated with actuation of an electric motor of the electric actuator, the actuation to cause a flow control member of the motor-operated valve to 

 move, the flow control member being mechanically coupled to the electric motor and being movable between an open position and a closed position; determining, by executing one or more instructions with the controller, an area under a torque curve based on the torque curve data; determining, by executing one or more instructions with the controller, a variance between the area under the torque curve and an area under a reference curve; determining, by executing one or more instructions with the controller, whether the variance exceeds a variance threshold; and generating a first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that the variance does not exceed the variance threshold. 10. The method of claim 9, further comprising presenting, via a user interface operatively coupled to the controller, a first notification indicating that the electric actuator is healthy, the first notification being based on the first control signal. 11. The method of any preceding claim, further comprising generating a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that the variance exceeds the variance threshold. 12. The method of any preceding claim, further comprising presenting, via the user interface, a second notification indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy, the second notification being based on the second control signal. 13. The method of any preceding claim, wherein the torque curve is partitioned into a breakout stage, a running stage, and an ending stage, the method further comprising: determining the area under the torque curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determining the variance between the area under the torque curve and the area under the reference curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determining whether the variance exceeds the variance threshold separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; and 

 generating the first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that none of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold. 14. The method of any preceding claim, further comprising generating a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that any of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold. 15. A non-transitory computer-readable storage medium comprising instructions that, when executed, cause a controller to at least: obtain torque curve data over a network from an electric actuator operatively coupled to the controller via the network, the torque curve data being associated with actuation of an electric motor of the electric actuator, the actuation to cause a flow control member of the motor-operated valve to move, the flow control member being mechanically coupled to the electric motor and being movable between an open position and a closed position; determine an area under a torque curve based on the torque curve data; determine a variance between the area under the torque curve and an area under a reference curve; determine whether the variance exceeds a variance threshold; and generate a first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that the variance does not exceed the variance threshold. 16. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of claim 15, wherein the instructions, when executed, are to further cause a user interface operatively coupled to the controller to present a first notification indicating that the electric actuator is healthy, the first notification being based on the first control signal. 17. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of any preceding claim, wherein the instructions, when executed, are further to cause the controller to generate a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that the variance exceeds the variance threshold. 18. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of any preceding claim, wherein the instructions, when executed, are further to cause the user interface to present a second notification indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy, the second notification being based on the second control signal. 19. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of any preceding claim, wherein the torque curve is partitioned into a breakout stage, a running stage, and an ending stage, and wherein the instructions, when executed, are to further cause the controller to: determine the area under the torque curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determine the variance between the area under the torque curve and the area under the reference curve separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; determine whether the variance exceeds the variance threshold separately for each of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage; and generate the first control signal indicating that the electric actuator is healthy when it is determined that none of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold. 20. The non-transitory computer-readable storage medium of any preceding claim, wherein the instructions, when executed, are to further cause the controller to generate a second control signal indicating that the electric actuator is unhealthy when it is determined that any of the respective variances of the breakout stage, the running stage, and the ending stage exceed the variance threshold.
